observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Chairperson of the Bids and
Awards Committee (BAC) and the BAC Secretariat to:

a. Ensure that the APPs for CY 2025 and in the succeeding years are under the
requirements under GPPB Circular No. 07-2015;

b. Prepare and submit the approved PMR within fourteen (14) calendar days after the
end of each semester, following the format prescribed by the GPPB, copy furnish
this Office for review and audit; and

c. Prospectively, strictly adhere to the provisions of RA No. 9184 and its 2016 Revised
IRR and of GPPB Circular No. 07-2015

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:

a. Facilitate the immediate submission of the lacking documents indicated in Audit
Observation Memorandum No. 25-001 (2024) issued on February 25, 2025.
Otherwise, the Audit Team may be constrained to issue a Notice of Suspension or
Notice of Disallowance for these transactions; and

b. Ensure that procurements made through the alternative modes of negotiated
procurement are supported with complete supporting documents as prescribed
under Appendix A of Annex "H" of the 2016 revised IRR of RA No. 9184 and COA
Circular No. 2012-001, to ascertain the propriety, validity and regularity of the
transactions.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to
immediately submit the 25 DVs and their supporting documents, totaling P4,446,254.79,
to the Office of the Auditor. Otherwise, the transactions may be subject to audit
suspension.

Furthermore, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal
Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to assign a designated focal person from each
office to monitor, reconcile, and facilitate the timely submission of DVs and their
supporting documents to the Office of the Auditor, pursuant to Section 7.2.1 of COA
Circular No. 2009-006.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the MSWDO to:

a. Develop and implement comprehensive guidelines on the eligibility criteria,
amount, and frequency of financial assistance, aligned with DSWD MC No. 15,
Series of 2022 and DSWD MC No. 06, Series of 2023, and formalized through the SB
Resolution to ensure transparency, accountability, and fairness in fund allocation.

b. Establish effective control measures to prevent the repeated granting of financial
assistance to the same individuals within short intervals unless duly justified by
supporting documentation and clear criteria.

Furthermore, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal
Accountant and MSWDO to enforce strict compliance with documentary requirements
by ensuring that all financial aid requests are supported with complete and verified
documents before approval and disbursement.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and
Municipal Treasurer to:

a. Formulate and install controls over cash through strict monitoring of bank balances
and timely updates of the actual flow of receipts and disbursements to help
determine whether sufficient funds are available to settle obligations and avoid
incurring overdrafts.

b. Take appropriate action to address the observed cash deficit through sound cash
management practice to ensure that adequate funds are available for all authorized
disbursements. Otherwise, necessary measures shall be undertaken to hold those
responsible for the incurrence of such overdrafts accountable.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer to:

a. Strictly enforce the timely deposit of collections, ensuring that all collections turned
over by the Liquidating Officer are deposited intact on the day of receipt or no later
than the next banking day, as required by Section 32, Chapter 3, Volume I of the
MNGAS for LGUs; and

b. Expedite the application and approval of bonds for deputized collectors, ensuring
compliance with Treasury Circular No. 02-2009 and PD No. 1445. Moving forward,
ensure that only bonded officers are authorized to handle municipal collections.

We reiterated our previous year’s audit recommendation that the Municipal Mayor
direct the GAD Focal Person to:

a. Propose a training program on the HGDG tool in the next GPB and/or seek the
assistance of a gender analysis specialist to ensure the proper use and
administration of the HGDG; and

b. Ensure that all succeeding GAD ARs include the required attachments, namely: a
summary of the reported program or project, copies of reported policy issuances,
results of HGDG tests, and actions taken by the LGU on COA audit findings and
recommendations. These attachments must be submitted within five (5) working
days from the end of January of the ensuing year, in accordance with Section V of
COA Circular No. 2016-01, to facilitate the thorough evaluation and assessment of
GAD PPAs undertaken by the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Budget Officer to:

a. Ensure that any adjustments in the Municipality's actual Internal Revenue Allotment
share are properly reflected in the 20 percent DF through a Supplemental Budget,
in compliance with Section 287 of RA No. 7160;

b. Submit a copy of the augmentation or supplemental budget records for obligations
that exceeded the corresponding appropriation, totaling P2,467,822.25. Otherwise,
the transactions may be subject to disallowance in audit; and

c. Implement and strictly enforce budget monitoring controls to prevent obligations
from exceeding available appropriations, ensuring adherence to Section 336 of RA
No. 7160.

Additionally, we reiterated our previous year's audit recommendation for the Municipal
Planning and Development Coordinator to:

a. Identify specific PPAs to be funded under the 20 percent DF; and

b. Ensure that itemized appropriations are included in the LDP and AIP, in compliance
with Article 454, Item (d) of the IRR of RA No. 7160.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Disaster Risk
Reduction and Management Officer and Municipal Budget Officer to stop the practice
of releasing and utilizing prior years’ unexpended LDRRMF without the required
recommendation from the LDRRMC and approval from the Sangguniang Bayan.
Additionally, future disbursements must strictly adhere to the guidelines set forth in
NDRRMC-DBM-DILG JMC No. 2013-001 and COA Circular No. 2012-002 to ensure
compliance, transparency, and accountability in the utilization of disaster-related
funds.

Record the receipt of RPT and SET from the taxpayers and subsequent distribution in accordance with Section 36 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I; and b. Direct the Municipal Treasurer to furnish the Municipal Accountant with a duly certified list showing the name of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year to serve as the basis for the set-up of the RPT/SET receivable.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our previous year's recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct: a. The Municipal Accountant to ensure that expenses are properly recorded in the appropriate accounts in accordance with the Revised Chart of Accounts prescribed in COA Circular No. 2015-009; and b. Henceforth, the OIC – Municipal Budget Officer to adopt the account titles and descriptions contained in the Annex B of COA Circular NO. 2015-009 in the budget preparation to avoid lump-sum appropriation for OMOE pursuant to LBC No. 112.
observations.managementAction:
Expenses were properly recorded under the appropriate accounts, in accordance with the Revised Chart of Accounts prescribed in COA Circular No. 2015-009; and
Furthermore, the account titles and descriptions provided in COA Circular No. 2015-009 were adopted during budget preparation to avoid lump-sum OMOE, in compliance with LBC No. 112.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require: a. The Municipal Treasurer to ensure the submission of Report of Unreleased Checks to the Office of the Municipal Accounting Office to establish the correct balances of receivables/payables accounts reported in the financial statements; and b. Moving forward, in case of the presence of stale checks, comply with the requirement of Paragraph 59, Volume I of the MNGAS for LGUs by sending a written notice to the payees of the checks payable due them at least one month before the checks become stale; and c. Revert to the Cash and Cash Equivalent accounts the amount of P1,029,871.85 and recognize the corresponding accounts Payables, Inter-Agency Payables, Other Payables and Trust Liabilities accounts which were understated by P308,171.32, P410,081.07, P283,971.46, and P27,648.00, respectively; and d. Evaluate the nature of the identified stale checks and make the necessary adjustments in the books to correct the balance of the Cash and Cash Equivalent accounts reported in the Financial Statements.
observations.managementAction:
Since the transactions were already settled upon receipt of the Audit Observation Memoranda (AOM), the preparation of the Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to revert and recognize the appropriate accounts can no longer be effected.
The Municipal Treasurer furnished the Municipal Accounting Office with the Report of Unreleased Checks as of year-end.
Furthermore, the Municipal Accountant prepared and implemented the Journal Entry Voucher to revert and recognize the appropriate accounts as per the Audit Observation Memoranda.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal General Services Officer – Designate to closely coordinate and immediately verify, analyze, and reconcile the discrepancy between the book balance and the RPCPPE to identify errors/deficiencies and make the necessary adjustments every time these are detected/discovered.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal General Services Officer – Designate has submitted a revised RPCPPE to the Office of the Auditor. This revision stemmed from the reconciliation of records between the Municipal Accounting Office and the Municipal General Services Office, indicating an error in the proper treatment of the project 'Repainting of the Municipal Hall' with a total contract cost of P828,380.40. The Building account mistakenly included this project in the initially submitted RPCPPE. The revised RPCPPE addressed these discrepancies.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to reclassify the portion of the long-term loan which is due for settlement beyond one year from reporting date as non-current liabilities for fair presentation of the account in the Statement of Financial Position.
observations.managementAction: The Enhanced Electronic New Government Accounting System (eNGAS) does not have a feature that allows for the separation of the current and non-current portions of long-term Loan Payable. This concern was raised with the Regional Office of the Commission through the Provincial Accounting Office, which assisted the Municipality in launching the system. For CY 2023, a disclosure was included in the Notes to Financial Statements to present the current and non-current portions of the long-term Loan Payable. For CY 2024, the current and non-current portions of the Loans Payable account will likewise be properly disclosed in the Notes to Financial Statements.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Environment and Natural Resources Officer (MENRO) and the Chairperson of the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to immediately submit the following documents otherwise the transactions shall be suspended in audit: a. Procurement and Bidding Documents (Legal, Technical and Financial Documents of bidders); b. Copy of the approved contract; c. Appropriate approved documents containing the terms and conditions, whether operated or bare rental for heavy equipment; whether per trip or package deal; and other relevant condition; d. The designated dumpsite/location of dumpsite; e. Estimated volume in cubic meters of garbage to be hauled from area of operation, including the basis for such estimates; and f. Record of Attendance/Service. Additionally, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure that payments of tipping fee for the disposal of Solid Waste Materials must be supported with complete documentation required under Sections 9.0 and 9.3 of COA Circular No. 2023-004.
observations.managementAction: As an immediate action to address the audit recommendation, the CY 2024 contract, together with the supporting procurement and bidding documents, was duly submitted to the Office of the Auditor. Compliance with Sections 9.0 and 9.3 of COA Circular No. 2023-004 was strictly monitored by the Municipal Accountant to ensure that payments for tipping fees related to the disposal of solid waste materials were supported by complete documentation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a. Submit a written explanation as to why the procurement of gasoline for privately owned motor vehicles amounting to P2,034,244.67 should not be disallowed in audit; b. Instruct his Executive Assistant to immediately submit the Monthly Report of Official Travels and the Monthly Report on Fuel Consumption for CY 2023; otherwise, the total disbursements for the gasoline amounting to P6,163,856.70 shall be suspended in audit; c. Adopt/Install a sound system of internal control on the procedure in the issuance, monitoring of gasoline expense; and d. Henceforth, stop the practice of issuing gasoline to privately owned motor vehicles and ensure that only government-owned motor vehicles are issued gasoline for official business. Furthermore, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure that claims for gasoline expenses must be supported with complete documentation required under COA Circular No. 77-61.
observations.managementAction: In compliance with the audit recommendation, a written explanation was prepared and submitted to justify the procurement of gasoline amounting to P2,034,244.67 for motor vehicles with plate numbers not registered under the Municipality. The written explanation detailed the necessity and specific circumstances surrounding these transactions. A summary of trips and a monthly report on the issuance of fuel to various offices and/or constituents were submitted to the Office of the Auditor. Additional controls have been instituted in the issuance of gasoline to strengthen internal control and accountability. However, some disbursement vouchers (DVs) were found to be incomplete due to absence of required signatories, indicating weak internal control measures. Despite prior audit recommendations, issuance of gasoline to privately owned motor vehicles continued during the first semester of CY 2024. Moving forward, stricter validation procedures prior to issuance of official receipts, enhanced coordination between collection and treasury sections, and regular audit of issued receipts versus actual payments received will be implemented.
observations.reason:
Not Implemented
The transaction documents and reports attached to the Disbursement Vouchers (DVs) were found to be incomplete due to the absence of required signatories for proper validation and approval.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal General Services Officer (MGSO) – Designate to: a. Maintain the following records: i. SPC for each class of semi-expendable property to properly record their issuance, transfers and disposal of the assets; ii. ICS to establish accountability over the government properties assigned to individual government personnel or offices; and iii. RegSPI per fund to record the issuance, return, reissuance, reissuance, disposal and other pertinent information about the property if returned and the description. b. Prepare the Report of RSPI and submit the same to the MAO, which shall serve as the basis in the preparation of the Journal Entry Voucher to recognize the semi-expendable property issued; c. Immediately submit to this Office the RPCSP for the CY 2023; and d. Henceforth, comply with the regulatory period of submission of RPCSP and strictly adhere to the issuance and maintenance of ICS provided for in the COA Circular No. 2022-004. Furthermore, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to maintain a SPLC to record the acquisition, description, custody, impairment, issue/transfer/disposal, repair history, and other information about the property.
observations.managementAction: The MGSO – Designate prepared and implemented SPCs for each class of semi-expendable property to properly record their issuance, transfer, and disposal. ICS were also issued to establish accountability over government properties assigned to individual offices. The RPCSP for CY 2023 was submitted to the Office of the Auditor. For CY 2024, the preparation and submission of the RPCSP and the issuance and maintenance of ICS were strictly adhered to by the MGSO – Designate. Additionally, the Municipal Accountant maintained a SPLC in compliance with the audit recommendations. The SPLC was used to record the acquisition, description, custody, impairment, issuance, transfer, disposal, repair history, and other relevant information concerning semi-expendable properties. A RegSPI per fund was prepared and updated to reflect the issuance and return of properties.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer to evaluate the necessity of the following: a. Designating additional DOs to eliminate the granting of multiple CAs to the DOs; and b. Increasing the amount of bond as a result of the increase in the accountability of the DO. Furthermore, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to: a. Deny request for additional CA if the DO has an existing unliquidated CA; b. Install, implement and monitor a sound system of internal control in granting and subsequent liquidation of CAs; and c. Henceforth, strictly comply with the provisions of PD No. 1445, COA Circular No. 97-002 and Treasury Circular No. 02-2009 on the granting, utilization and liquidation of CA.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer segregated the nature of cash advances by assigning cash advances for Miscellaneous Expenses to herself and those for payroll and honorarium to Ms. Leila B. Valeraso, as reflected in Office Order Nos. 06 and 08 issued by the Municipal Mayor. Management installed, implemented, and continues to monitor a sound system of internal control over the granting, utilization, and subsequent liquidation of Cash Advances (CAs). A key example of this internal control is the segregation of duties as established by the Office Order Nos. 06 and 08, which assign responsibility for different types of CAs to designated officers. The Municipal Accountant implemented procedures to deny additional CAs to DOs with unliquidated CAs and monitor the timely liquidation of CAs.
observations.reason:
Not Implemented
The bond amount remains unchanged despite the increased accountability of the DOs. Multiple CAs were still granted to a DO during CY 2024.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Human Resource Management Officer and Municipal Accountant to stop processing multiple loans beyond the financial capacity of the employees causing them to receive Net Take Home Pay (NTHP) below Five Thousand Pesos (P5,000.00).
observations.managementAction: Employees with a Net Take-Home Pay (NTHP) below P5,000.00 were not granted additional loans. Some employees continue to have an NTHP below the prescribed threshold due to the ongoing repayment of loans that had already been granted prior to the implementation of the audit recommendation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct: a. The Municipal Budget Officer and GFPS to ensure that the appropriation for GAD Fund is at least 5 percent of the total annual budget pursuant to Section 4.1.C.1 of PCW – DILG – DBM – NEDA JMC No. 2013-01; and b. The GAD Focal Point System (GFPS) to conduct a gender analysis through the administration of the HGDG tool during the identification, design, implementation, and monitoring stages of GAD PPAs. Also, propose an Executive Order/Administrative Order creating a Monitoring and Evaluation Team to evaluate outcomes of GAD policies, programs and projects in consonance with Section 5.1 of PCW – DILG – DBM – NEDA JMC No. 2013-01.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Budget Officer, in coordination with the GFPS, ensured that the appropriation for the GAD Fund is maintained at a minimum of five percent of the total annual budget, in compliance with Section 4.1.C.1 of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01. Subsequently, the results of the HGDG assessment together with the supporting documents (e.g., project proposal/brief) shall be attached to the GPB for submission to the Office of the Auditor. The GFPS is to conduct gender analysis using the HGDG tool. No Executive Order or Administrative Order creating a Monitoring and Evaluation Team has been issued by the Municipal Mayor as of the audit period.
observations.reason:
Not Implemented
No Executive Order or Administrative Order creating a Monitoring and Evaluation Team has been issued by the Municipal Mayor as of the audit period. The required gender analysis using the HGDG tool was not yet conducted.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Planning and Development Officer to: a. Identify specific PPAs to be funded out of the 20 percent DF and to include the itemized appropriations in the Local Development Plan and AIP pursuant to Article 454, Item (d) of the IRR of RA No. 7160; and b. Secure first the approval or authorization from the Local Sanggunian prior to implementation of specific PPAs under the 20 percent DF funded out of lump-sum appropriation pursuant to Section 3.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 as clarified in COA Memorandum No. 2010-014. In addition, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure that the procurement of infrastructure projects are duly supported with complete documentation provided for in COA Circular Nos. 2012-001 and 2023-004 pursuant to Section 4 (6) of PD No. 1445.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Mayor directed the Municipal Planning and Development Officer (MPDO), who concurrently serves as the OIC – Municipal Budget Officer, to immediately submit to the Office of the Auditor a written justification on why the aforementioned PPAs under the 20 percent DF were entered and executed and related disbursing expenditures were disbursed without prior approval from the Sangguniang Bayan. The justification detailed the reasons for entering into contracts and disbursing funds for the implementation of PPAs under the 20 percent DF without prior approval from the Sangguniang Bayan. SB Resolutions No. 86 and 100, Series of 2024, pertaining to fund realignment/reprogramming were submitted; however, these did not cover all reprogramming necessary to address the overdrafts. Management committed to strengthen budget monitoring processes.
observations.reason:
Not Implemented
SB Resolution No. 86 did not include any provision or reference to the reprogramming of appropriations. SB Resolution No. 100 addressed only specific PPAs and did not cover reprogramming for Other Infrastructure/Public Infrastructure Assets and the Water Supply System, which still reflect obligations in excess of appropriations totaling P2,467,822.25.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor to direct the Municipal Accountant to: a. Verify the unremitted amount of P6,364.88 or effect the necessary adjustment, if warranted; b. Analyze and reconcile the individual SL accounts with abnormal debit balance and draw the necessary adjustments; and c. Strictly monitor the monthly preparation of the withholding tax and for remittance to ensure that the balance of Due to BIR is nil after remittance.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant provided a written explanation. The amount of P6,364.88 was immediately remitted to the BIR. The abnormal balance of P43,333.55 noted in the Subsidiary Ledger represents employee refunds due to suspension from service and the end of term of elected officials following the recently concluded Barangay and Sangguniang Kabataan Elections. The said abnormal balance was corrected as of January 2024.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to: a. Provide a written explanation as to why payments for the employees’ PhilHealth premiums and GSIS premiums and loan amortizations; b. Immediately remit the PhilHealth premiums withheld totaling P1,766.39; and c. Henceforth, strictly monitor the monthly withholding and remittance of PhilHealth and GSIS premiums and/or loan amortizations to avoid incurrence of interest and/or penalties.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant provided a written explanation which disclosed that the over/under remittance reflected in each account was primarily caused by timing differences due to delayed processing of salaries for casual employees. The unremitted amount of P1,766.39 was immediately remitted to PhilHealth. Management committed to proactively monitor monthly withholding and remittance of PhilHealth and GSIS premiums and loan amortizations to avoid penalties.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Human Resource Management Officer to: a. Provide a written explanation as to why payments for the employees’ loan amortization were not deducted from their monthly salaries; and b. Stop the practice of extending credit to Municipal employees to settle their loan amortizations. Also recommended that the Municipal Accountant strictly monitor the monthly withholding and remittance of HDMF premiums and loan amortizations to avoid incurrence of interest and/or penalties.
observations.managementAction: A written explanation was submitted which disclosed that the Municipality had a longstanding practice of providing assistance to employees in settling their loan amortizations. Despite the Municipal Accountant’s limited ability to intervene—due to compassion for the employees and directives from the Local Chief Executive—proactive measures have been undertaken. The Municipality committed to stop the practice of extending credit to employees for loan amortizations.
observations.reason:
Not Implemented
The practice of extending credit to the Municipal employees for settlement of their loan amortizations was still evident for CY 2024.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
 a. Municipal Treasurer to furnish the Municipal Accountant with a duly certified list showing the name of taxpayers, indicating therein the property classification, and the amount due and collectible for the year; and
 b. Municipal Accountant: ➢ To set-up the RPT/SET Receivable based on the duly certified list furnished by the Municipal Treasurer at the beginning of the year; ➢ To adopt the modified accrual method in accounting for RPT/SET income; and ➢ To draw the necessary adjusting JEV to correct any errors/discrepancies.
observations.managementAction:
For CY 2023, the Municipal Treasurer’s Office (MTO) furnished a document indicating Municipality’s Total Assessment Value and the Total Amount of RPT and SET collectible for the year to the Municipal Accounting Office (MAO). The amount contained in the said document served as basis for drawing JEV to record RPT and SET Receivables and related Deferred Income at the beginning of Calendar Year (CY) 2023; which then the Municipal Accountant correspondingly recorded with JEV Nos. JEV-2023-01-008313 and JEV-2023-01-000640.
However, the pertinent provision of MNGAS for LGUs provides that the duly certified list showing the name of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year shall be the basis in drawing the JEV to establish the RPT/SET Receivable at the beginning of the year. Due to the non-submission of the afore-mentioned document, the Audit Team was precluded from verifying the accuracy of the amount set-up for RPT/SET Receivable and its respective Deferred Income accounts.
The MAO established the RPT/SET receivable and its corresponding deferred income of P11,944,874.90, respectively, at the beginning of the year; however, as similarly noted in CY 2022 annual audit, review of the sampled JEV revealed that the MAO draws the following entry upon collection of RPT/SET: Account Debit Credit Cash in Local Treasury xxx Real Property Tax xxx Special Education Tax xxx Due to Local Government Units (LGUs) xxx
Corroboratively, as evidenced by the following General Ledgers (GL) / Subsidiary Ledgers (SL), the MAO does not adjust the respective receivable and deferred income accounts upon receipt of payment from the taxpayer.
GL and SL of RPT Receivable
JEV Date JEV No. Transaction Amount
01/03/2023 JEV-2023-01-008313 Set up of RPT Receivable at the beginning of the year 2023 P 11,944,874.90
12/29/2023 JEV-2023-12-008314 Correction of Subsidiary Ledgers to adjust the RPT Receivable account based on Certified List of all RPT Delinquencies for the CY 2023 134,363,666.43
GL and SL of SET Receivable
01/03/2023 JEV-2023-01-000640 Set up of SET Receivable at the beginning of the year 2023 P 11,944,874.90
12/29/2023 JEV-2023-12-000641 Correction of Subsidiary Ledgers to adjust the RPT Receivable account based on Certified List of all Real Property Tax Delinquencies for the CY 2023 134,363,666.43
12/29/2023 JEV-2023-12-000642 Correction of Subsidiary Ledgers to adjust the SET Receivable account based on Certified List of all Real Property Tax Delinquencies for the CY 2023 0.69
The foregoing practice is non-compliant with Section 20 of MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I, which explicitly provides that: “Upon collection of Real Property Taxes from taxpayers, the account Deferred Real Property Tax Income/Deferred Special Education Tax Income shall be debited while the Real Property Tax Income due to the municipality is recognized/credited. The share of the Province and Barangay shall also be credited to Due to LGUs xxx.”
As a result, the year-end balance of the RPT/SET Receivable accounts and its respective Deferred Income accounts were overstated by the total collected/realized income of P6,224,045.26, respectively.
The total balance presented in the MTO’s Certified List of all Real Property Tax Delinquencies in comparison with the year-end balances of the RPT/SET Receivables disclosed a discrepancy totaling P226,934,253.70. Details are as follows:
RPT SET Total
Per Statement of Financial Position P 149,478,767.65 P 149,478,767.65 P 298,957,535.30
Per Certified List of all Real Property Tax Delinquencies 36,011,640.80 36,011,640.80 72,023,281.60
Discrepancy P113,467,126.85 P113,467,126.85 P226,934,253.70
Absence of detailed SL impedes the conduct of reconciliation between the records of the respective offices. It is note-worthy that discrepancy between the aforementioned records was also noted in the previous year’s annual audit. Further, the discrepancy has significantly increased from P84,617,366.13 to P226,934,253.70 or 268.19 percent. Based on the foregoing, the Municipality was not able to address the deficiency; hence, the non-implementation of the prior year’s audit recommendations.
observations.reason:
The Municipal Planning and Development Officer commented that some lots are inundated. Albeit the existence of Tax Declarations to show ownership over the parcels of lot, the physical location, and even the location in the cadastral map cannot be determined. This hampered the accurate determination of RPT/SET receivables and the effective monitoring of payments received.
The MAO traced the discrepancy between and the year-end balances causing the adjustments made in the foregoing accounts under JEV Nos. 2023-12-008314 and 2023-12-000641 amounting to P134,363,666.43, respectively.
Likewise, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to reconcile/trace the material discrepancy amounting P84,617,366.13 between the year-end balances of RPT/SET Receivables account reported in the SFP by the Municipal Accounting Office and the total amount disclosed in Certified List of all Real Property Tax Delinquencies submitted by the Municipal Treasurer’s Office.
The MAO traced the discrepancy between the year-end balances of RPT/SET receivables account in the SFP and the amount disclosed in the Certified List of Real Property Tax Delinquencies. However, the amount set-up by the MAO was based on MTO’s Total Assessment Value instead of the duly certified list.
The discrepancy has significantly increased from P84,617,366.13 to P226,934,253.70 or 268.19 percent; hence, the audit recommendation was not addressed by the Management.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct:
 a. The Municipal Accountant to ensure that expenses are properly recorded in the appropriate accounts in consonance with the revised chart of accounts prescribed in COA Circular No. 2015-009; and
 b. Henceforth, the OIC – Municipal Budget Officer to adopt the account titles and descriptions contained in the Annex B of COA Circular No. 2015-009 in the budget preparation to avoid lump-sum appropriation for OMOE pursuant to LBC No. 112.
observations.managementAction: The MAO records expenditures based on the account codes provided in the Obligation Request from the Municipal Budget Office (MBO). Moving forward, the MAO and MBO will closely coordinate to ensure that expenses are properly recorded in the appropriate accounts, in consonance with the revised chart of accounts prescribed in COA Circular No. 2015-009.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to reclassify the portion of the long-term loan which is due for settlement beyond one year from reporting date as non-current liabilities for fair presentation of the account in the Statement of Financial Position.
observations.managementAction: At present, eNGAS does not include a feature that separates the current and non-current portions of long-term Loan Payable. This concern was raised with the Regional Office of the Commission thru the Provincial Accounting Office, which assisted the Municipality in launching the system. For the time being, a disclosure in the Notes to Financial Statements has been provided to present the current and non-current portion of long-term Loan Payable.
observations.reason: As of this writing, the reclassification has not been facilitated; however, the Audit Team noted Management’s comment and will monitor and validate its compliance in the succeeding audit year.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Planning and Development Officer, and in his capacity as the OIC – Municipal Budget Officer, to immediately submit to the Office of the Auditor a written justification on why contracts for the implementation of the aforementioned PPAs under the 20 percent DF were entered and executed and related expenditures were disbursed without prior approval from the Sangguniang Bayan.
 Henceforth, we recommend that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
 ● Municipal Planning and Development Officer to: a. Identify specific PPAs to be funded out of the 20 percent DF and include the itemized appropriations in the LDP and AIP pursuant to Article 454, Item (d) of the Implementing Rules and Regulations (IRR) of RA No. 7160; and b. Secure first the approval or authorization from the Local Sanggunian prior to implementation of specific PPAs under the 20 percent DF funded out of lump-sum appropriation pursuant to Section 3.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 as clarified in COA Memorandum No. 2010-014.
 ● Municipal Accountant to ensure that procurement of infrastructure projects are duly supported with complete documentation provided for in COA Circular Nos. 2012-001 and 2023-004 pursuant to Section 4 (6) of PD No. 1445.
observations.managementAction:
In a letter dated April 22, 2024, the Municipal Planning and Development Officer averred that he inherited the practices of the Municipality. However, the Municipal Planning and Development Office (MPDO) will ensure that projects are not implemented without prior approval from the Sangguniang Bayan.
In a separate letter also dated April 22, 2024, the Municipal Accountant stated that she agreed to comply with the audit recommendations and ensured that, henceforth, procurement of infrastructure projects will be duly supported with complete documentation provided in COA Circular Nos. 2012-001 and 2023-004 pursuant to Section 4(6) of PD No. 1445.
observations.reason: The justification provided by the MPDO is inadequate. The observation regarding lump-sum appropriation was noted and included in the CY 2022 Annual Audit Report. Therefore, the audit recommendation on itemized appropriation should have been implemented, and deficiencies should have been corrected.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our audit recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct the GAD Focal Person to strictly adhere with Item V of COA Circular No. 2014-001 on the submission of the GAD Plan and Budget and GAD Accomplishment Report for the timely review and evaluation of the implementation of the proposed GAD programs, projects and activities.
observations.managementAction: Copies of the GPB and GAD AR, supported by a certificate of review and approval of the Provincial DILG was forwarded to the Audit Team on May 12, 2023 in response to the audit observation. In CY 2023 the GPB was duly submitted to the Audit Team; however, the GAD AR was submitted on March 15, 2024 or beyond the reglementary period under Section V of COA Circular No. 2014-001.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to:

a. Record the receipt of payment of RPT and SET from the taxpayers and subsequent
distribution of collections in accordance with Section 36 of the MNGAS for LGUs,
Volume I; and
b. Draw the necessary adjusting JEV to correct any errors/discrepancies.

Furthermore, we reiterated our previous year’s audit recommendations that the
Municipal Mayor:

a. Direct the Municipal Treasurer to furnish Municipal Accountant with a duly certified list
showing the name of taxpayers, indicating therein the property classification, and the
amount due and collectible for the year; and
b. Instruct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to reconcile/trace the
noted material discrepancy amounting to P226,934,253.70 between the year-end
balances of RPT/SET Receivables account reported in the SFP by the Municipal
Accounting Office and the total amount disclosed in Certified List of all Real
Property Tax Delinquencies submitted by the Municipal Treasurer’s Office.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct:

a. The Municipal Accountant to ensure that expenses are properly recorded in the
appropriate accounts in consonance with the revised chart of accounts prescribed in
COA Circular No. 2015-009; and

b. Henceforth, the OIC – Municipal Budget Officer to adopt the account titles and
descriptions contained in the Annex B of COA Circular No. 2015-009 in the budget
preparation to avoid lump-sum appropriation for OMOE pursuant to LBC No. 112.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require that:

● The Municipal Treasurer

a. Ensure the submission of Report of Unreleased Checks as of year-end to the
Municipal Accounting Office to establish the correct balances of receivables/payables
accounts reported in the financial statements; and

b. Moving forward, in case of the presence of stale checks, comply with the
requirement of Paragraph 59, Volume I of the MNGAS for LGUs by sending a
written notice to the payees of the existence of checks payable due them at least one
month before the checks become stale;

● The Municipal Accountant

a. Revert to the Cash and Cash Equivalent accounts the amount of P1,029,871.85 and
recognize the corresponding Payables, Inter-Agency Payables, Other Payables and
Trust Liabilities accounts which were understated by P308,171.32, P410,081.07,
P283,971.46, and P27,648.00, respectively; and

b. Evaluate the nature of the identified stale checks and make the necessary
adjustments in the books to correct the balance of the Cash and Cash Equivalent
accounts reported in the Financial Statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant and
Municipal General Services Officer – Designate to closely coordinate and immediately
verify, analyze, and reconcile the discrepancy between the book balance and the MGSO
records to identify the causes of the errors/deficiencies noted and make the necessary
adjustments every time these are detected/ discovered.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to
reclassify the portion of the long-term loan which is due for settlement beyond one
year from reporting date as non-current liabilities for fair presentation of the account
in the Statement of Financial Position.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the designated Municipal
Environment and Natural Resources Officer (MENRO) and the Chairperson of the Bids
and Awards Committee (BAC) to immediately submit the following documents
otherwise the transactions shall be suspended in audit:

a. Procurement and Bidding Documents (Legal, Technical and Financial Documents
of bidders);
b. Copy of the approved contract;
c. Appropriate approved documents containing the terms and conditions, whether
operated or bare rental for heavy equipment; whether per trip or package deal; and
other relevant condition;
d. The designated dumpsite/location of dumpsite;
e. Estimated volume in cubic meters of garbage to be hauled from area of operation,
including the basis for such estimates; and
f. Record of Attendance/Service

Accountant to ensure that payments of tipping fee for the disposal of Solid Waste
Materials must be supported with complete documentation required under Sections
9.0 and 9.3 of COA Circular No. 2023-004.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

a. Submit a written explanation as to why the procurement of gasoline for privately
owned motor vehicles or plate numbers not registered under the Municipality totaling
P2,034,244.67 should not be disallowed in audit;

b. Instruct his Executive Assistant to immediately submit the Monthly Report of Official
Travels and the Monthly Report on Fuel Consumption for CY 2023; otherwise, the total
disbursements for gasoline amounting to P6,163,856.70 shall be suspended in audit;

c. Adopt/Install a sound system of internal control on the procedure in the issuance,
control and monitoring of gasoline expense; and

d. Henceforth, stop the practice of issuing gasoline to privately owned motor vehicles and
ensure that only government-owned motor vehicles are issued gasoline for official
business.

Furthermore, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal
Accountant to ensure that claims for gasoline expenses must be supported with complete
documentation required under COA Circular No. 77-61.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal General Services
Officer – Designate to:

a. Maintain the following records:

i. SPC for each class of semi-expendable properties to record issuance, transfers
and disposal of the assets;
ii. ICS to establish accountability over the government properties; and
iii. RegSPI per fund to record the issuance, return, reissuance, disposal and other
information about the property if returned and the description.

b. Prepare the Report of RSPI and submit the same to the MAO, which shall serve as
basis in the preparation of the Journal Entry Voucher to recognize the semi-
expendable property issued;

c. Immediately submit to this Office the RPCSP for the CY 2023; and

d. Henceforth, comply with the regulatory period of submission of RPCSP and
strictly adhere to the issuance and maintenance of ICS provided for in the COA
Circular No. 2022-004.

Furthermore, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal
Accountant to maintain a SPLC to record the acquisition, description, custody,
impairment, issue/transfer/disposal, repair history, and other information about the
property.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer to evaluate
the necessity of the following:

a. Designating additional DOs to eliminate the granting of multiple CAs to the DO;
and

b. Increasing the amount of bond as a result of the increase in the accountability of
the DO.

Furthermore, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal
Accountant to:

a. Deny request for additional CA if the DO has an existing unliquidated CA;

b. Install, implement and monitor a sound system of internal control in granting and
subsequent liquidation of CAs; and

c. Henceforth, strictly comply with the provisions of PD No. 1445, COA Circular No.
97-002 and Treasury Circular No. 02-2009 on the granting, utilization and
liquidation of CA.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Human Resource Management
Officer and Municipal Accountant to stop processing multiple loans beyond the
financial capacity of the employees causing them to receive Net Take Home Pay of
below Five Thousand Pesos (P5,000.00).

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct:

a. The Municipal Budget Officer and GFPS to ensure that the appropriation for GAD Fund
is at least 5 percent of the total annual budget pursuant to Section 4.1.C.1 of PCW – DILG
– DBM – NEDA JMC No. 2013-01; and

b. The GFPS to conduct a gender analysis through the administration of the HGDG tool
during identification, design, implementation, management, and monitoring and
evaluation stages of the GAD PPAs. Subsequently, the results of the HGDG assessment
together with the supporting documents (e.g., project proposal/brief) shall be attached
to the GPB for submission to the Office of the Auditor;

Also, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor to issue an Executive Order or an
Administrative Order creating a Monitoring and Evaluation Team, which shall evaluate
the outcomes of the GAD policies, programs and projects in consonance with Section
5.1 of PCW – DILG – DBM – NEDA JMC No. 2013-01.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Planning and
Development Officer, and in his capacity as the OIC – Municipal Budget Officer, to
immediately submit to the Office of the Auditor a written justification on why contracts

entered and executed and related expenditures were disbursed without prior approval

Henceforth, we recommend that the Municipal Mayor direct the:

● Municipal Planning and Development Officer to:

itemized appropriations in the LDP and AIP pursuant to Article 454, Item (d) of the
Implementing Rules and Regulations (IRR) of RA No. 7160; and

b. Secure first the approval or authorization from the Local Sanggunian prior to
implementation of specific PPAs under the 20 percent DF funded out of lump-sum

● Municipal Accountant to ensure that procurement of infrastructure projects are duly
supported with complete documentation provided for in COA Circular Nos. 2012-001
and 2023-004 pursuant to Section 4 (6) of PD No. 1445.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor to direct the Municipal Accountant to:

a. Verify the unremitted amount of P6,364.88 and immediately remit or effect the
necessary adjustment, if warranted;

b. Analyze and reconcile the individual SL accounts of Due to BIR with abnormal debit
balance and draw the necessary adjustments; and

c. Strictly monitor the monthly preparation of withholding tax and due for remittance to
ensure that the balance of Due to BIR is nil after remittance.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:

a. Provide a written explanation on the occurrence of over and/or under-remittance of
employees PhilHealth premiums and GSIS premiums and loan amortizations;

b. Immediately remit the PhilHealth premiums withheld totaling P1,766.39; and

c. Henceforth, strictly monitor the monthly withholding and remittance of PhilHealth
and GSIS premiums and/or loan amortizations to avoid incurrence of interest and/or
penalties.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Human Resource Management
Officer, Municipal Treasurer, and Municipal Accountant to:

a. Provide a written explanation as to why payments for employees’ loan amortization
were not deducted from their monthly salaries; and

b. Stop the practice of extending credit to Municipal employees to settle their loan

Accountant to strictly monitor the monthly withholding and remittance of HDMF
premiums and loan amortizations to avoid incurrence of interest and/or penalties.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require that:
 ● The Municipal Treasurer

 b. Evaluate the nature of the identified stale checks and make the necessary adjustments in the books to correct the balance of the Cash and Cash Equivalent accounts reported in the Financial Statements.

The procurement of gasoline for vehicles not registered under the Municipality of Lumban occurs due to the following instances: • Assisting dialysis patients from the Municipality who need to travel to Dialysis Centers in Laguna and Batangas for their regular sessions when Municipal ambulances are unavailable; • Addressing requests of Civil Service Organizations (CSOs) and Non-Governmental Organizations (NGOs) frequently invited to attend meetings and conferences outside the Municipality; • Providing fuel for grass cutters and motorized bancas in their regular clean-up operations along the roads and Lumban Rivers, as well as for work operations conducted by the Water Tender Group; • Assisting the Municipal Fisheries and Aquatic Resources and Management Council (MFARMC) in their regular monitoring and operation against illegal fishing along the Lumban River and the peripheral area of Laguna de Bay; • Providing assistance to constituents in urgent or emergency situations, such as transporting family members or relatives to hospitals; and • Supplying gasoline to employees of Post Office, Lumban Waterworks System, Municipal Agriculture Office, Rural Health Unit, Municipal Engineering Office and Lumban Public Market who use their own motorcycles/vehicles for fieldwork assignments.
Following the discussion of the matter during the Exit Conference, the Municipality will refrain from issuing gasoline to privately-owned vehicles and barangay patrols without plate numbers. Nonetheless, the Municipality ensures that there is no loss of public funds and no government funds were used for private purposes.
In addition, The Municipal Accountant agreed to comply with the audit recommendation and ensured that, henceforth, claims for gasoline expenses will be supported with complete documentation.
observations.reason: The Audit Team noted Management’s comment, and compliance with the audit recommendation will be strictly monitored and validated in the succeeding audit year. Additionally, it is emphasized that the Monthly Report of Official Travels and the Monthly Report on Fuel Consumption for CY 2023 remain unsubmitted as of writing.

observations.recommendation:

surcharges that may be imposed by BIR for late/non-filing of returns.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor enjoin the immediate submission of
the list of the specific programs, projects and activities implemented under the 20
per cent Development Fund in CY 2021 to verify whether the charging of those
PPAs is valid and accurate in accordance with the provisions of DILG and DBM
JMC No. 2017-1.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Disaster Risk
Reduction and Management Officer (MDRRMO) to continue optimizing the
utilization of the LDRRM Fund and further strengthen its capacity to mitigate
disaster risk in order to ensure and maintain the maximum implementation of
disaster mitigation and preparedness programs/projects/activities (PPAs), under
LDRRMF.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the GAD Focal Person to:
a. strictly adhere to Item V of COA Circular No. 2014-001 dated March 18, 2014
for the timely submission of the GAD Plan and Budget and GAD
Accomplishment Report to the COA;
b. comply with the submission of the GPB and GAD AR to the Provincial
Planning and Development Office and the DILG Provincial Office for their
review, endorsement and consolidation as provided under PCW-DILG-DBM-
NEDA JMC Nos. 2016-01 and 2013-01; and
c. submit immediately the Utilization Report for CY 2021 to the Office of the
Auditor for evaluation of the expenses incurred.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require:
a. The Municipal Accountant and Municipal General Services Officer to reconcile PPE records and reconcile the discrepancies noted between the RPCPPE and the Accounting records to effect the adjustment to arrive at reconciled balances and to maintain updated PEC and PPELCs as basis for reconciliation and validation; and
b. The Municipal Accountant to reclassify the PPE totaling P4,013,407.65 to appropriate PPE accounts according to its classification, specification and nature; and
c. The Municipal Accountant to draw a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to drop the small value property items from the books by debiting Prior Years' Adjustment of P118,693.16 and Accumulated Depreciation of P324,864.83 and by crediting the respective PPE accounts totaling P443,557.99.
observations.managementAction: The two offices committed to reconcile the RPCPPE and Accounting records submitted for reporting in the CY 2021 Financial Statements. However, no reason was provided to the Audit Team for the inability to reconcile the two records. The Municipal Accountant has drawn a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to reclassify the subject amount to the appropriate PPE accounts. The Municipal Accountant has drawn a JEV to recognize expense upon issuance of the semi-expendable PPE items and to adjust both the PPE accounts and Government Equity.
observations.reason: Variances were still noted between the RPCPPE and Accounting Schedules. Some of the noted semi-expendable tangible items were still carried in the books as PPE as of December 31, 2021.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Assessor to facilitate the titling of the subject lots, as well as the transfer of title of lots not yet registered under the name of the Municipality, in compliance with Section 148 of COA Circular No. 92-386 and Section 47 of the IRR of the Local Government Code of 1991 to avoid contingent claims and third-party disputes.
( Note: This recommendation text appears mismatched in the table; intended recommendation was to draw JEVs to adjust trust liabilities including transfer of unexpended LDRRMF and reconcile balances.)
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Assessor coordinated to ensure that the reciprocal balances appearing in the books of both Local Government Units (LGUs) are reconciled. The necessary adjustments to reconcile the reciprocal accounts have been made to reflect the correct balance in the Financial Statements. The Municipal Accountant has drawn JEVs to transfer prior year's unexpended LDRRM balance to the Trust Liabilities – DRRM account.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our audit recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to coordinate with the Provincial Accountant and reconcile the balance of Due from LGU appearing in the books of the Municipality against the balance of Due to LGU appearing in the books of the Province. We also recommended that necessary adjustment be made upon reconciliation to correct the discrepancy and arrive at an accurate and correct balance of the account in the Financial Statements.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant coordinated with the Provincial Accountant and reconciled the reciprocal balances appearing in the books of the Provincial Government. The necessary adjustments to reconcile the reciprocal accounts have been made to reflect the correct balance in the Financial Statements.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the immediate liquidation by the concerned Municipal officer and employee of the outstanding cash advances amounting P63,620.00 and henceforth, ensure that all cash advances are fully liquidated at the end of the year in compliance with COA Circular No. 97-002.
observations.managementAction: The outstanding cash advances amounting P63,620.00 were immediately liquidated on the next succeeding quarter of CY 2021.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to coordinate with the OIC – Municipal Budget Officer in the preparation and submission of the Statement of Comparison of Budget and Actual Amounts (SCBAA) to reflect the correct balances of actual expenditures and unexpended portions of the Municipality's funds.
observations.managementAction: The Accounting and Budget Offices coordinated to present accurate balances of actual obligation as presented in the SCBAA.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant in coordination with other concerned officials to: a. initiate action in cleaning the dormant receivables in the books of account in accordance with COA Circular No. 2016-005; b. complete the processing of requirements for the filing of request for authority to write-off the receivables totaling P196,900.00 and submit the same documents to the Audit Team for review and evaluation; and c. continue to exert effort for its collectability when circumstances would warrant.
observations.managementAction: The Accounting Office has prepared a JEV to derecognize the loans receivable in the amount of dormant loans receivables as reported in the books of accounts.
observations.reason: Basis for the derecognition of the loans receivables was not provided to the Audit Team. Copy of the JEV drawn was not provided. Documents showing collection of the subject receivables were said to be the basis for not requesting write-off, but these documents were not presented to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to facilitate the immediate submission of the 11 DVs and their supporting documents. Henceforth, require submission of accounts within the period prescribed under Section 100 of PD No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 2009-006. The remedies provided under Section 122 prescribed by COA Circular No. 2009-006 may be availed of in instances of unjustified failure to submit said accounts.
observations.managementAction: Of the noted 11 DVs, eight were immediately submitted to the Office of the Auditor. The Municipality committed to process and pay the remaining DVs to the Office of the Auditor within the prescribed time.
observations.reason: Three DVs (one payable to Marilag Rice Mill and two for M. C. Dela Cruz Enterprises and general Contractor) remained unsubmitted as of December 31, 2021. No reason was provided for the inability to submit.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require: a. the Municipal Accountant to record purchases of office supplies, accountable forms, supplies, drugs and medicines, medical, dental and laboratory supplies, food supplies and other supplies as debit to appropriate asset account using perpetual inventory system regardless of whether they were consumed within the accounting period; maintain subsidiary ledger cards for each supplies and materials; and record issuances based on RSMI; and b. the Municipal General Services Officer to maintain stock cards and property cards for supplies; prepare and submit monthly RSMI to the Municipal Accountant for recording of issuances; and prepare and submit the Report on the Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI) by type of inventory as of a given date.
observations.managementAction: The Accounting Office has recognized purchases into the proper inventory account upon recording and has recognized the entire amount as expense at the end of the month, since all supply items were immediately issued to the end-user. The General Services Office has started maintaining updated stock cards and submitted copies of RSMI to the Accounting Office as basis for recording issuances. The GSO prepared the RPCI and submitted the same to the Office of the Auditor on February 7, 2022.
observations.reason: The per item recording was not yet achieved due to lack of manpower to handle the task. The submitted RSMI and RPCI lacked details such as stock number and unit value, failing to report the actual cost of the remaining inventories at year-end.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the GSO to cause the disposal of property reported as unserviceable in the IIRUP in accordance with Section 79 of PD No. 1445 and Section 170 to 180 of the Manual on the Rules and Procedures on Management of Property for LGUs to decongest the area where these properties were stockpiled and recognize possible income from disposition. Upon disposal, direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare Journal Entry Voucher to derecognize these properties and adjust PPE accounts as required under IPSAS No. 17.
observations.managementAction: The Inventory and Disposal Committee has already disposed some of the PPE items reported unserviceable through sale, transfer to other offices, and destruction. The Municipal General Services Office provided the Accounting Office with a copy of the Waste Material Report (WMR) to be used as basis for derecognition of the disposed properties.
observations.reason: There are remaining unserviceable PPE items yet to be disposed as of December 31, 2021. The Accounting Office indicated difficulty in tracing sold spare parts to specific PPE items, delaying derecognition.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the General Services Officer to submit copies of Contracts, POs and supporting documents to the Office of the Auditor once these documents are perfected to facilitate timely inspection. Likewise, furnish the Auditor delivery documents as required under COA Circular No. 2009-002.
observations.managementAction: The GSO submitted copies of contracts and POs to the Office of the Auditor once these documents were perfected; delivery documents were likewise submitted to the Office of the Auditor to facilitate timely conduct of inspection of the deliveries.
observations.reason: There were still delays noted in the submission of delivery documents and requests for inspection in some instances.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Assessor to facilitate the titling of the subject lots and the transfer of title of lots not yet registered under the name of the Municipality in compliance with Section 148 of COA Circular No. 92-386 and Section 47 of the IRR of the Local Government Code of 1991 to avoid contingent claims and third-party disputes.
observations.managementAction: Two parcels of lot with total cost of P68,200.00 were titled in CY 2021. Another two parcels totaling P264,070.00 were requested for titling in the first quarter of CY 2022. The Municipal Assessor’s Office is currently locating copies of Deed of Donation for the transfer of titles of the other seven lots.
observations.reason: Clarifications are still needed to process the titling of road lots based on guidelines issued by the DENR.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to ensure that disbursement vouchers are processed and paid with complete documentation to establish the propriety of expenses in compliance with Section 9.1.3.2 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 and to submit the lacking documents listed in Appendix O to comply with documentary requirements.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Government partially submitted the noted insufficient documents.
observations.reason: There are outstanding lacking documents which were still not provided.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to deduct applicable withholding taxes for every payment of goods and services procured by the Municipality as required under Section 5.116 of BIR Regulation No. 2-98 for remittance on due date to avoid penalties and surcharges. Also, refrain from allowing claims for payment of expenses on a reimbursement basis and ensure that checks are paid directly to whom the payment is due.
observations.managementAction: The Management tried to limit claims for reimbursements during CY 2021 and committed to require payments to be made directly to suppliers where feasible.
observations.reason: Audit of CY 2021 disclosed reimbursements to officials and employees totaling P973,610.03 which were not deducted with withholding taxes.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor and the Municipal Accountant refrain from granting additional cash advances to any official or employee without liquidating first his/her prior cash advance in compliance with COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997.
observations.managementAction: Additional cash advances were not granted unless prior cash advances were liquidated.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our audit recommendations that the Municipal Mayor: a. take legal steps to enforce settlement of unliquidated Cash Advances; b. implement COA Circular No. 90-329 by initiating immediate filing of appropriate criminal proceedings against the defaulting accountable officers; and c. exhaust all possible steps to facilitate the settlement of accountabilities of the deceased former Municipal Treasurer.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant informed the Audit Team of the approval of Sangguniang Bayan Resolution No. 48, S. 2021 dated June 22, 2021, authorizing the Local Chief Executive to enter into a Memorandum of Agreement with a legal consultant. The Management has planned to initiate filing legal proceedings, but unavailability of personal details of the accountable officers hindered proceedings.
observations.reason: The unavailability of personal details of the accountable officers such as current address or contact information hindered the proceedings to pursue legal action.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Local Development Council to continue its commitment to maximize the implementation of the development projects under the 20 percent Development Fund and enjoin the immediate utilization and implementation of the remaining unimplemented projects in order to fully achieve 100 percent realization and completion of development projects. Also submit the Detailed Utilization Report for the 20 percent Development Fund to the Office of the Auditor for evaluation of the expenses incurred.
observations.managementAction: Copy of the CY 2020 Utilization Report was submitted to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to transfer the unexpended balance of the QRF and LDRRMF-MOOE at year-end to the Special Trust Fund of the Trust Fund books, in compliance with COA Circular No. 2012-002.
observations.managementAction: A total amount of P2,000,000.00 was reported transferred to the Trust Liabilities – DRRM account. The remaining amount pertaining to understatement of expenses was reported as continuing appropriation during CY 2021.
observations.reason: The Municipal Accountant explained that P2,000,000.00 was transferred while a remaining amount P62,259.01 was reported as continuing appropriation during CY 2021.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our audit recommendation that the Municipal Mayor reorganize the MSWMC and conform with the membership and requirements in accordance with Rule VI of the IRR of RA No. 9003.
observations.managementAction: Action has been undertaken to reorganize the MSWMC. Reorganization document for CY 2022 was prepared.
observations.reason: Copies of the reorganization document for CYs 2020 and 2021 were not submitted to the Audit Team; reorganization was effected in CY 2022.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our audit recommendation that the Municipal Mayor: a. direct the GAD Focal Point Designate to monitor the strict implementation of plans, programs and activities included in the GPB and fully utilize the GAD Budget to achieve its goal of promoting gender-responsive governance; and b. require the GAD Focal Point Designate to submit GAD Accomplishment Report with sufficient documentation.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Government registered a high implementation rate of the GAD Fund in CY 2021; the GAD Focal Point Designate coordinated with MPDO/OIC-Budget and other officials to ensure proposed PPAs under the GAD Fund will not exceed the allocated budget.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our audit recommendation that the Municipal Mayor instruct the GAD Focal Point Designate, MPDO/OIC-Budget and all concerned officials to align the PPAs included in the GAD Plan and Budget with the allocated total budget of GAD Fund based on five percent of the Annual Budget.
observations.managementAction: The GAD Focal Point Designate coordinated with the MPDO/OIC-Budget Officer and other officials to ensure that proposed PPAs under the GAD Fund will not exceed the allocated five percent of the total budget of the Municipality for CY 2021.
