We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. require the Municipal Accountant to maintain updated PPE Ledger Cards; and
b. direct the Municipal General Services Officer (MGSO) to conduct annual physical inventory and submit the required RPCPPE.
observations.managementAction:
The Accounting Office has planned to update the PPE Ledger alongside the periodic updating of the depreciation schedule for the PPE of the Municipality.
The MGSO has conducted the annual physical inventory for CY 2020 and submitted the required RPCPPE to the Audit Team on February 15, 2021.
observations.reason: The lack of manpower and the adoption of alternative work arrangement affected the implementation of this recommendation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to reconcile their records and determine the causes of discrepancies and draw Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) and require the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to maintain Property Ledger Cards and Property Cards, respectively, in accordance with Sections 119 and 120 of MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I so that the correctness and reliability of the reported amount in the financial statements can be fully ascertained.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer coordinated and were able to reconcile their records. The Accounting Office and the Treasury has agreed to update the records once the reconciliation has been completed.
observations.reason: The reconciliation of records took time since both parties needed to secure copies of proof of ownership. The work from home system and the alternative work arrangement also affected the reconciliation and updating of records.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to classify the outstanding balance of the long-term loans payable by presenting portion of loans due for settlement within twelve months as part of the current liabilities and the remaining amount which is due for settlement beyond one year as non-current liabilities in the Statement of Financial Position.
observations.managementAction: The Accounting Office has provided for the necessary adjustment to reflect the correct balance of Loans Payable, while Loans to be settled within twelve months was presented separately from its non-current portion in the Statement presented Financial Statements.

The Treasury Office, in coordination with the Office of the Municipal Assessor, was able to update the Municipality’s List of Assessed Real Property Tax, showing the RPT and SET receivables from each property owner for the CY 2020.
The updated List of Assessed Real Property Tax was provided by the Treasury Office to the Accounting Office, which was used as basis for recording the CY 2020 RPT and SET Receivables.
The Municipal Accountant has drawn the necessary JEV at the beginning of the year to recognize RPT and SET Receivables, which was reflected in the Financial Statements for CY 2020.
The Municipal Accountant has planned to coordinate with the Office of the Municipal Assessor as regards to the update of Delinquency Reports to be used as basis for the recognition of RPT and SET receivable accruing from prior Years.
observations.reason: The updated List of Assessed Real Property Tax was provided and the JEV was drawn; however, the adoption of alternative work arrangement hampered the recording of Delinquency Reports to be used as basis for the recognition of prior years receivables.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to draw Journal Entry Vouchers to adjust the account in the General Fund and Trust Fund books, thus reflect the correct balance of Trust Liabilities - DRRMF.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant has drawn the necessary entry to record the transfer of prior years’ unutilized balance of the fund to the Trust Liability – DRRM under the Trust Fund books.
observations.reason: The determination of the year-end balance of unutilized LDRRMF to be transferred to the Trust Liabilities - DRRM account was affected by the late submission of the last quarter's gasoline expenses to be obligated to the LDRRMF, thus the inability to immediately transfer the balance to the Trust Liabilities - DRRM account.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. require the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Engineer to reconcile their records to determine the status and correct balances of the affected CIP accounts; 
b. advise the Municipal Engineer to provide the Accounting Office with copies of the report of on-going projects, its percentage of completion and the completed projects to cause the transfer of these projects from the CIP accounts to the appropriate PPE account and the Registry of Public Infrastructure; and
c. advise the Municipal Accountant to effect the reclassification of the affected account to arrive at reliable and accurate amount for fair presentation in the Financial Statements.
observations.managementAction: The Accounting Office conducted periodic reconciliation with the Office of the Municipal Engineer. Reclassification was made and the completed projects were transferred from the CIP accounts to the appropriate PPE accounts and were recorded in the Registry of Public Infrastructure.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. complete the processing of requirements for the filing of request for authority to write-off the receivables totaling P196,900.00 and submit to the Auditor for review and evaluation; 
b. set up Allowance for Impairment for recognition has set up an amount of possible losses from the receivables amounting to P196,900.00 and effect the adjustment in the amount of asset account in the Statement of Financial Position for fair presentation; and 
c. continue to exert effort for its collectability when circumstances would warrant.
observations.managementAction: The Accounting Office has planned to complete the necessary requirements for the request for authority to write-off. The Accounting Office has set up an Allowance for Impairment and recognized impairment loss for outstanding receivables deemed uncollectible, as reflected in the CY 2020 Financial Statements. Efforts for its collectability were exerted but still to no avail.
observations.reason: The Management claimed that there was no turnover of documents required from the retired Municipal Agricultural Officer which hampered the process. Also, inability to obtain a complete listing of borrowers prevented the actual determination of the collectible amounts.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to draw the necessary Journal Entry Voucher to drop the small value property items from the books by debiting Prior Years’ Adjustment and Accumulated Depreciation of respective PPE, and by crediting the respective PPE accounts in the books of the General Fund and the Special Education Fund.
observations.managementAction: The necessary JEVs were drawn to drop the small value property items from the books – both for the General Fund and the Special Education Fund, and the net book value was recognized as a prior period adjustment.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to coordinate with the Provincial Accountant and reconcile the balance of Due from LGU appearing in the books of the Municipality against the balance of Due to LGU appearing in the records of the Provincial Government. Effect the necessary adjustment upon reconciliation to correct the discrepancy to arrive at an accurate and correct balance of the account in the Financial Statements.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant coordinated with the Provincial Accountant to verify the unreconciled balances for CY 2019, and records of both offices were adjusted accordingly. Upon reconciliation, the Accounting Office corrected the CY 2019 balance of Due from LGU account.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer, and the Municipal Assessor to facilitate the titling of the subject lots as well as the transfer of title still in process in compliance with Section 148 of COA Circular No. 92-386 and Section 47 of the IRR of the Local Government Code of 1991 to avoid contingent claims and third-party disputes.
observations.managementAction: During CY 2020, the Government was able to process the transfer of title of 14 lots under the name of Mr. Jose Raul Abellada to the name of the Municipality. The necessary fees had been paid to the Provincial Treasurer’s Office and as of year-end, the Municipality awaits the release of the TCTs from the Registry of Deeds. This reduced the number of lots for transfer of titles from 23 to nine.
observations.reason: The delays were caused by restrictions imposed due to the COVID-19 Pandemic and the adoption of alternative work arrangement by not only the Municipal Government, but also by other government agencies involved in the processing of titles.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to facilitate the immediate submission of the 20 DVs and their supporting documents. Henceforth, require submission of accounts within the period prescribed under Section 100 of PD No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 2009-006. The remedies provided under Section 122 of PD No. 1445 may be availed of in instances of unjustified failure to submit said accounts.
observations.managementAction: The Accounting Office has submitted the Disbursement Vouchers and their supporting documents to the Office of the Auditor. The Accounting Office partially submitted the DVs to the Office of the Auditor.
observations.reason: Some suppliers give Official Receipts, Collection Receipts, and other supporting documents beyond the prescribed time for submission, resulting in the delays in submission.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our previous year’s recommendations that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. Municipal Accountant:
i. adopt the Perpetual Inventory Method in accounting for the receipts and issuances of supplies, in compliance with regards to Sections 114 and 121 of MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I; 
ii. record the issuances thereof based on the Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI) submitted by the Supply Officer; and 
iii. maintain the supplies ledger cards for each of the items of supplies and materials procured.
b. Supply Officer to:
i. prepare and submit the Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI) together with the original copy of the Requisition and Issue Slips to the Municipal Accountant on a weekly basis; ii. maintain stock cards; and iii. conduct physical count of supplies at least every semester and prepare and submit the Report on the Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI) to the Office of the Auditor not later than July 31 and January 31 for the first and second semesters respectively, to report the physical count of supplies by type of inventory.
observations.managementAction: The Accounting Office started to update its supplies ledger cards for each of the items of supplies procured. The General Services Officer submitted copies of the SSMI to the Accounting Office for the recognition of the issuance of supplies and materials. The GSO conducted physical count of Property, Plant and Equipment (PPE), which also included several supplies and materials categorized as inventories and semi-expendable items.
observations.reason: The lack of manpower and the adoption of alternative work arrangement affected the implementation of this recommendation. The Municipality issues two quarters worth of supplies in bulk due to no available stock room to store the supplies; hence stock cards were not updated as to remaining unused supplies held by different offices.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Appraisal and Disposal Committee to cause the disposal of property reported as unserviceable in the RPCPPE in accordance with Section 79 of PD No. 1445 to decongest the area where these properties were stockpiled and recognize possible income from the disposal thereof. Upon disposal, direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare Journal Entry Voucher in order to derecognize these properties and adjust the overstatement of PPE account in the books as required under IPSAS No. 17.
observations.managementAction: The General Services Officer mentioned that the Municipality has already created an Appraisal and Disposal Committee under Executive Order No. 188, s. 2021 effective April 26, 2021. The Management also committed to identify all unserviceable properties located in the different offices of the Municipality to complete the listing for preparation for appraisal. The GSO submitted a copy of the IIRUP to account for the unserviceable PPE items of the Municipality.
observations.reason: The delay in the approval of the proposed committee was due to several priority activities during the COVID-19 pandemic and the adoption of alternative work arrangement. The Management is still locating some of the reported properties since most were purchased years ago and might have already been infested by termites, etc.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our audit recommendations that the Municipal Mayor:
a. take legal steps to enforce settlement of unliquidated Cash Advances; 
b. implement COA Circular No. 90-329 by initiating immediately the filing of the appropriate criminal proceedings against the defaulting accountable officers; and 
c. exhaust all possible steps to facilitate the settlement of accountabilities of the deceased former Municipal Treasurer.
observations.managementAction: The Management explained that they cannot enter into a contract with a legal counsel unless it was approved by the Sangguniang Bayan (SB). As of year-end, the proposal is still for approval in the SB.
observations.reason: The delay in filing appropriate legal actions is due to awaiting Sanggunian Bayan approval to contract a legal counsel. Some demand letters were sent but some were returned due to incorrect addresses; efforts continue.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor and the Municipal Accountant refrain from granting additional cash advances to any official, officer or employee without liquidating first his/her prior cash advance in compliance with COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997.
observations.managementAction: The Accounting Office closely monitored the granting and liquidation of the cash advances, which also involved checking for unliquidated cash advance balances before granting another. The Management explained that some cash advances (e.g., for local travel) were granted before announcement of lockdown and were delayed in liquidation; also cash was given for immediate COVID-19 response where suppliers accepted cash only.
observations.reason: Delays in liquidation were due to COVID-19 lockdowns and emergency responses which required cash payments; management continues to monitor.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Budget Officer to allocate additional funding of P4,357,408.10 to back-up the cash insufficiency of the LDRRMF.
observations.managementAction: Monthly transfers to LBP Account No. 0242-1114-60 for the LDRRMF’s share from the income were made to ensure that all current appropriations are backed-up with availability. During CY 2020, the Municipality was able to back-up the unexpended 30 percent Quick Response Fund accruing from CY 2016. This cash was used to respond to the needs of the constituents during the COVID-19 pandemic.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to transfer the unexpended balance of the QRF and LDRRMF-MOOE totaling P1,748,191.55 to the Special Trust Fund of the Trust Fund books, in compliance with the COA Circular No. 2012-002.
observations.managementAction: Monthly transfers were made and during CY 2020, the Municipality was able to back-up the unexpended 30 percent Quick Response Fund accruing from CY 2016 which was used to respond to needs during the COVID-19 pandemic.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Local Development Council to continue its commitment to maximize the implementation of the development projects under the 20 percent Development Fund and enjoin the immediate utilization and implementation of the remaining unimplemented projects in order to fully achieve the 100 percent realization and completion of development projects.
observations.managementAction: As mandated under RA Nos. 11469 and 11494, the Local Government Units can realign programs, projects and activities (PPAs) caused by amendments for COVID-19 response PPAs instead, the Municipality availed the option and has utilized 82.60 percent of the total allotted DF by year-end of CY 2020.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct the MSWMC and the MENRO to review and update the Ten-Year Solid Waste Management Plan of the Municipality every two years or as the need arises, in compliance with Section 4 (g), Rule VI of the IRR of RA No. 9003.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Planning and Development Officer, who is currently the MENRO Designate, explained that the updating of the Municipal Solid Waste Management Plan (MSWMP) requires the conduct of Waste Analysis and Characterization Study (WACS), which is usually done by clustering the municipalities in the province. He also identified the COVID-19 pandemic as one of the reasons for the delay in the conduct of WACS.
observations.reason: The seminars and WACS were not conducted in CY 2020 due to restrictions brought by the COVID-19 pandemic which hampered the updating process.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our audit recommendation that the Municipal Mayor reorganize the Municipal Solid Waste Management Council (MSWMC) and conform with the membership requirements in accordance with the Rule VI of the IRR of RA No. 9003.
observations.managementAction: The MENRO Designate informed the Audit Team that EO No. 05A, s. 2021 entitled “Reconstitution of the Municipal Solid Waste Management Council of the Municipality of Lumban” was effected on January 11, 2021.
observations.reason: Delay in reorganization was due to restrictions brought by the COVID-19 pandemic.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the GAD Focal Point Designate, the Municipal Development and Planning Officer and all other officials concerned to align the Plans, Programs and Activities (PPAs) included in the GAD Plan and Budget (GPB) with the allocated GAD Fund based on five percent of the Annual Budget.
observations.managementAction: Coordination with the GAD Focal Point Designate and the Planning Officer was made in order to align the Plans, Programs and Activities (PPAs) included in the GAD Plan and Budget (GPB) with the allocated GAD Fund based on five percent of the Annual Budget.
observations.reason: No reason was cited by Management.

observations.managementAction: The GAD Focal Person monitored implementation of PPAs and coordinated with the Municipal Accountant. The GAD Focal Point Designate furnished the Office of the Auditor with a copy of the GAD Accomplishment Report. The Municipal Accountant coordinated with the GAD Focal Person to identify specific PPAs to which the expenses pertain.
observations.reason: Most activities were cancelled due to IATF community quarantine restrictions; documentation supporting some reported PPAs was insufficient. There was no designated alternate person to handle GAD Focal Person duties.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the GAD Focal Point System to:
a. coordinate with the concerned officials for the establishment of the GAD Database consisting of sex-disaggregated data and other information in accordance with PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01 July 18, 2013; and 
b. coordinate with the MPDO and other concerned offices to address the causes of errors and difficulties encountered during data gathering and formulate plans to correct them for ease in data collection procedures.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Planning and Development Officer (MPDO) and GAD Focal Point Designate explained that the Municipality continued gathering data needed for the establishment of GAD Database through the conduct of Community Based Monitoring System (CBMS) surveys, but activities were limited due to restrictions mandated by the community quarantine protocols, and the conduct of priority surveys mandated by the National Government.
observations.reason: The process of data gathering was halted and limited due to COVID-19 quarantine protocols and priority surveys mandated by the national government; no action undertaken to address data errors/difficulties as of reporting.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the MSWMB to review and update the Ten-Year Solid Waste Management Plan of the Municipality every two years or as the need arises in compliance with Section 4 (g), Rule VI of the IRR of RA No. 9003.
observations.managementAction: The Management explained that updating the Solid Waste Management Plan requires attending trainings and seminars and data such as waste characterization which require random collection and testing. One of the topics discussed during the latest quarterly meeting of the MSWMC is the updating of the MSWMP.
observations.reason: Data such as solid waste characterization, which is necessary for review and update of the existing Solid Waste Management Plan, requires more than two years of study and testing.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. require the Municipal Accountant to maintain updated PPE Ledger Cards;
and
b. direct the Municipal General Services Officer (MGSO) to conduct annual
physical inventory of the municipal property, and to prepare and submit
the required RPCPPE.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. direct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to reconcile
their records and determine the causes of discrepancies and draw the
necessary adjusting entries to reflect the correct balance of the Land
account; and
b. require the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to maintain
Property Ledger Cards and Property Cards, respectively, in accordance
with Sections 119 and 120 of MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I so that
correctness and reliability of the reported amount in the financial
statements can be fully ascertained.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to
classify the outstanding balance of the long-term loans payable by presenting the
portion of loans due for settlement within twelve months as part of the current
liabilities and the remaining amount which is due for settlement beyond one year
as non-current liabilities in the Statement of Financial Position.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require:
a. the Municipal Treasurer to update their List of Assessed Real Property
Tax to show the balance of collectible taxes per property;
b. the Municipal Treasurer to furnish the Municipal Accountant with a duly
certified list showing the name of the taxpayers and the amount due and
collectible for the year;
c. the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to coordinate in
recording the amount of Real Property Tax and Special Education Tax
Receivables accruing from prior years; and
d. the Municipal Accountant to draw a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) at the
beginning of the year by debiting Real Property Tax Receivable/Special
Education Tax Receivable and crediting Deferred Real Property Tax
Income/Deferred Special Education Tax Income based on the duly
certified list provided from the Municipal Treasurer for fair presentation of
the accounts in the Statement of Financial Position.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to draw
Journal Entry Vouchers to adjust the account in the General Fund and Trust Fund
books, thus reflect the correct balance of Trust Liabilities - DRRMF.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. require the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Engineer to reconcile
their records to determine the status and correct balances of the affected
CIP accounts;
b. advise the Municipal Engineer to provide the Accounting Office with
copies of the report of on-going projects, its percentage of completion
and the completed projects to cause the transfer of these projects from
the CIP accounts to the appropriate PPE account and the Registry of
Public Infrastructure; and
c. advise the Municipal Accountant to effect the reclassification of the
affected account to arrive at reliable and accurate amount for fair
presentation in the Financial Statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and
other officials concerned to:
a. complete the processing of requirements for the filing of request for
authority to write-off for the receivables totaling P196,900.00 and submit
to the Auditor for review and evaluation;
b. set up Allowance for Impairment for recognition of possible losses from the
receivables amounting to P196,900.00 and effect the adjustment in the amount
of asset account in the Statement of Financial Position for fair presentation; and
c. continue to exert effort for its collectability when circumstances would
warrant.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to draw the
necessary Journal Entry Voucher to drop the small value property items from the books
by debiting Prior Years’ Adjustment and Accumulated Depreciation of respective PPE,
and by crediting the respective PPE accounts in the books of the General Fund in the
amounts of P155,609.23, P475,395.47 and P631,004.60, respectively; and in the books
of Special Education Fund in the amounts of P11,001.01, P139,053.99 and
P150,055.00, respectively.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to
coordinate with the Provincial Accountant and reconcile the balance of Due from LGU
appearing in the books of the Municipality against the balance of Due to LGU
appearing in the records of the Provincial Government. Effect the necessary
adjustment upon reconciliation to correct the discrepancy to arrive at an accurate and
correct balance of the account in the Financial Statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer, and the
Municipal Assessor to facilitate the titling of the subject lots as well as the transfer of
title still in process in compliance with Section 148 of COA Circular No. 92-386 and
Section 47 of the IRR of the Local Government Code of 1991 to avoid contingent
claims and third-party disputes.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and
the Municipal Treasurer to facilitate the immediate submission of the 20 DVs and
their supporting documents. Henceforth, require submission of accounts within the
period prescribed under Section 100 of PD No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 2009-006.
The remedies provided under Section 122 of PD No. 1445 may be availed of in
instances of unjustified failure to submit said accounts.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendations that the Municipal Mayor
direct the:
a. Municipal Accountant to:
i. adopt the Perpetual Inventory Method in accounting for the receipts
and issuances of supplies, in compliance with Sections 114 and 121
of MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I;
ii. record the issuances thereof based on the Summary of Supplies and
Materials Issued (SSMI) submitted by the Supply Officer; and
iii. maintain the supplies ledger cards for each supplies and materials
for reconciliation with the stock cards of the Supply Officer.
b. Supply Officer to:
i. prepare and submit the Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued
(SSMI) together with the original copy of the Requisition and Issue
Slips to the Municipal Accountant on a weekly basis;
ii. maintain stock cards; and
iii. conduct physical count of supplies at least every semester and
prepare and submit the Report on the Physical Count of Inventories
(RPCI) to the Office of the Auditor not later than July 31 and January
31 for the first and second semesters respectively, to report the
physical count of supplies by type of inventory.

We reiterated our audit recommendation that the Municipal Mayor create an
Appraisal and Disposal Committee to appraise various unserviceable Property,
Plant and Equipment for disposal.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the said Committee to cause the
disposal of property reported as unserviceable in the RPCPPE in accordance with
Section 79 of PD No. 1445 to decongest the area where these properties were
stockpiled and recognize possible income from the disposal thereof.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. take legal steps to enforce settlement of unliquidated Cash Advances;
b. implement COA Circular No. 90-329 by initiating immediately the filing of
the appropriate criminal proceedings against the defaulting accountable
officers; and
c. exhaust all possible steps to facilitate the settlement of accountabilities of
the deceased former Municipal Treasurer.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor and the Municipal
Accountant refrain from granting additional cash advances to any official, officer or
employee without liquidating first his/her prior cash advance in compliance with COA
Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and the General Services Officer to reconcile the discrepancy between their records, and effect the necessary adjustments for fair presentation of the PPE accounts.
observations.managementAction: The Management ensured the required Subsidiary Ledgers are reconciled and adjustments were reflected in the books. The reconciliation between the Accounting and GSO records are on-going.
observations.reason: The Accounting Office and General Services Office were not able to completely reconcile their PPE records.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our previous year’s recommendations that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. Municipal Accountant to:
i. adopt the Perpetual Inventory Method in accounting for the receipts and issuances of supplies, in compliance with Sections 114 and 121 of MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I; ii. record the issuances thereof based on the Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI) submitted by the Supply Officer; and iii. maintain the supplies ledger cards for each supplies and materials for reconciliation with the stock cards of the Supply Officer.
b. Supply Officer to:
i. prepare and submit the Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI) together with the original copy of the Requisition and Issue Slips to the Municipal Accountant on a weekly basis; ii. maintain stock cards; and iii. conduct physical count of supplies at least every semester and prepare and submit the Report on the Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI) to the Office of the Auditor not later than July 31 and January 31 for the first and second semesters respectively, to report the physical count of supplies by type of inventory.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant plans to coordinate with the Municipal General Services Office to resolve the matter as soon as possible.
observations.reason: Only one SSMI was furnished to the Accounting Office due to the one-time issuance of all supplies to requesting offices which hindered the continuous preparation of the required documents. The GSO lacks manpower who will trace all unused supplies that are already issued to requesting offices.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to: a. account properly for the principal and interest payments and recognize the principal and interest portions of the loan payments. b. draw a Journal Entry Voucher to adjust the outstanding balance of Loans Payable-Domestic account and recognize the Interest Expense in prior years.
observations.managementAction: Adjustments at year-end were prepared to reflect principal and interest portions of the loan payments. A Journal Entry Voucher was drawn to adjust the outstanding balance of Loans Payable – Domestic Account and recognize the Interest Expense from prior years.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to maintain Subsidiary Ledgers that show the details or breakdown of the balances in the General Ledger and the Financial Statements.
observations.managementAction: The Management ensured the maintenance of the required Subsidiary Ledgers.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to facilitate the immediate submission of the 14 DVs and their supporting documents, otherwise, impose the sanctions provided under Section 122 of PD No. 1445.
observations.managementAction: The Management submitted the 14 DVs with their respective supporting documents.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to take appropriate action on the causes of the discrepancy between the reciprocal accounts Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds and make the necessary adjustments to bring both accounts in agreement.
observations.managementAction: The Accounting Office prepared reconciliation and the adjustments noted were taken into account.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Budget Officer to include the cost of titling of the purchased lots in the annual budget. We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor assign personnel to facilitate the transfer of titles of the acquired land to protect the interest of the government.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Mayor assigned the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Assessor to facilitate the transfer of titles. The Management included the cost of titling of the purchased lots in the CY 2020 Annual Budget under the Office of the Municipal Assessor.
observations.reason: The allocated budget was not enough to cover the cost of titling.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to exert effort to collect the overdue receivables, otherwise, evaluate the possible applicability of COA Circular No. 2016-005 relative to dormant receivables accounts. The Management should send demand letters to the concerned individuals for the collection of the outstanding receivables.
observations.managementAction: The Management has sent demand letters to the concerned individuals for the collection of the overdue receivables and coordinated with concerned agencies for the required documentation to be able to request for write-off.
observations.reason: The Department of Agriculture (DA) has yet to respond to the request of the Municipality.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our previous recommendations that the Municipal Mayor require the immediate liquidation by concerned Municipal employees of the outstanding cash advances amounting P6,900.00 and henceforth ensure that all cash advances are fully liquidated at the end of the year in compliance with COA Circular Nos. 97-002; ensure that cash advance is only granted to bonded employees and increase the fidelity bond of the Municipal Disbursing Officer to cover cash advances; and that the Municipal Mayor and the Municipal Accountant refrain from granting additional cash advances to any official, officer or employee without liquidating first his/her prior cash advance in compliance with COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997.
observations.managementAction: No cash advances remained unliquidated at year-end. The Municipal Disbursing Officer’s fidelity bond was increased during the second semester of CY 2019. The Management ensured that cash advances granted with unliquidated balances have to be settled first before the grant of another cash advance.
observations.reason: The Office of the Auditor was not furnished with a copy of the Disbursing Officer's fidelity bond for CY 2018. The Accounting Office overlooked the granting of certain cash advances which were treated as payment instead of cash advance.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our audit recommendation that the Municipal Mayor create an Appraisal and Disposal Committee to appraise various unserviceable Property, Plant and Equipment for disposal. We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the said Committee to submit to the Office of the Auditor a request for disposal of all unserviceable property together with the Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property (IIRUP), Appraisal Report and other required documents for appropriate action.
observations.managementAction: The Audit Team has been informed by the Management that the composition of the Appraisal and Disposal Committee was already submitted to the Office of the Municipal Mayor and is awaiting review and approval. The Management also committed to comply with the audit recommendation on the disposal of unserviceable PPE.
observations.reason: Data such as date of acquisition and unit cost of some of the unserviceable properties cannot be traced due to lack of turnover from the previous General Services Officer.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that the GAD Focal Point in coordination with the Local Planning and Development Offices establish GAD Database as required under Section 4.1.B of JMC No. 2013-01 of the PCW, DILG, DBM and NEDA.
observations.managementAction: The Management is already in the process of collecting the necessary information through the conduct of the Community-Based Monitoring System (CBMS). The personnel in-charge have interviewed about 1,000 households out of 8,000 or approximately 12.5 percent as of August 20, 2020.
observations.reason: The CBMS data gathering requires substantial time and resources; data collection was only partially completed (approximately 12.5 percent as of date).

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the GAD Focal Person to indicate the actual results vis a vis the target when preparing the GAD Accomplishment Report and to comply with the reporting guidelines of Joint Circular No. 2004-1 of DBM, NEDA and NCRFW to ensure proper evaluation of the accomplishment report.
observations.managementAction: The GAD Focal Person has submitted the GAD Accomplishment Report indicating the actual results vis-à-vis the targets.
observations.reason: No separate documentation was prepared to prove actual conduct of the Plans, Programs and Activities (PPAs).

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to determine and address the cause/s of the discrepancy in reporting the unutilized balance of the LDRRMF between the Report on the LDRRMF Utilization, the Statement of Financial Position and the Notes to Financial Statements.
observations.managementAction: The Management has reviewed the existing report and explained the reason for the insufficient LDRRMF back up of cash. The late transfer of the unutilized LDRRMF for the current year is due to the timing of the computation for the actual realized income.
observations.reason: The late transfer of the unutilized LDRRMF for the current year is due to the timing of the computation for the actual realized income which usually is after the reporting period. Prior years' balances of unutilized LDRRMF did not have a proper turnover.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer to explain why the Cash in Bank balance as of December 31, 2018 of the LDRRMF including the Trust Fund are insufficient and to take appropriate action otherwise, ensure sufficient funding.
observations.managementAction: The Management has explained the reason for the insufficient cash back up of the LDRRMF. Monthly transfers were made to the LDRRMF bank account in LBP to ensure that current appropriations and planned PPAs are backed-up with sufficient funding.
observations.reason: More than 50 percent of the insufficiency can be traced to the unutilized LDRRMF from prior years which lacked proper turnover from the previous accountant.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the LCPC be reconstituted to include representatives from the youth and the private sector in compliance with Section 15 of RA No. 9344.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality reconstituted the LCPC in Lumban through the issuance of an Executive Order in CY 2019.
observations.reason: The copy of the submitted Executive Order was unnumbered, undated and unsigned.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer remit immediately in full the taxes withheld in prior periods and take appropriate measures to prevent recurrence.
observations.managementAction: Tracing and reconciliation made by the Accounting Office revealed that there was an error in the recording of Due to BIR. It was corrected thru a Journal Entry Voucher. All taxes withheld were fully remitted on January of the succeeding year. The error in the Due to BIR account has been corrected and the remaining balance was remitted by the Municipality through EFPS on January 10, 2020.
observations.reason: It was corrected through a Journal Entry Voucher and subsequent remittance.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal
Accountant and the General Services Officer to reconcile the discrepancy between
their records, and effect the necessary adjustments for fair presentation of the PPE
account balances in the Statement of Financial Position.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendations that the Municipal Mayor
direct the:
a. Municipal Accountant to:
 adopt the Perpetual Inventory Method in accounting for the receipts and
issuances of supplies, in compliance with Sections 114 and 121 of
MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I;
 record the issuances thereof based on the Summary of Supplies and
Materials Issued (SSMI) submitted by the Supply Officer; and
 maintain the supplies ledger cards for each supplies and materials for
reconciliation with the stock cards of the Supply Officer.

b. Supply Officer to:
 prepare and submit the Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI)
together with the original copy of the Requisition of Issue Slips to the
Municipal Accountant;
 maintain stock cards; and
 conduct physical count of supplies and prepare and submit the Report
on the Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI) to the Office of the Auditor to
report the physical count of supplies by type of inventory.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:
 account properly for the principal and interest portion of the loan payments
and recognize accrued interest in the books; and
 draw a Journal Entry Voucher to adjust the outstanding balance of Loans
Payable-Domestic account and the non-recognition of Interest Expense in
prior years as follows:
Particulars Debit Credit Amount
To correct outstanding balance of Prior Loans P2,871,471.35
Loans Payable-Domestic account Period Payable-
for CY 2018 and to recognize prior Adjustment Domestic
years interest expense

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to
maintain Subsidiary Ledgers that show the details or breakdown of the balances
in the General Ledger and the Financial Statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to
facilitate the immediate submission of the 14 DVs and their supporting documents,
otherwise, impose the sanctions provided under Section 122 of PD No. 1445.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal
Accountant to take appropriate action on the causes of the discrepancy between the
reciprocal accounts Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds and make the
necessary adjustments to bring both accounts in agreement.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Budget Officer to
include the cost of titling of the purchased lots in the name of Municipality of
Lumban in their annual budget. We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor
assign personnel to facilitate the transfer of titles of the acquired land to protect
the interest of the government.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to
exert effort to collect the overdue receivables, otherwise, evaluate the possible
applicability of COA Circular No. 2016-005 relative to dormant receivables
accounts.

a. We reiterated our previous recommendation that the Municipal Mayor require
the immediate liquidation by concerned Municipal employees of the
outstanding cash advances amounting P6,900.00 and henceforth ensure that
all cash advances are fully liquidated at the end of the year in compliance with
COA Circular Nos. 97-002.

b. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and the Municipal Accountant
ensure that cash advance is granted only to bonded officers/employees.
We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal
Treasurer to increase the amount of bond of the Municipal Disbursing Officer
to P750,000 to cover her cash advances which had reached P1,785,105.42 as of
year-end.

c. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and the Municipal Accountant
refrain from granting additional cash advances to any official, officer or
employee without liquidating first his/her prior cash advance.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor create an Appraisal
and Disposal Committee to appraise various unserviceable Property, Plant and
Equipment for disposal.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the General Supply Officer
(GSO) to submit to COA Auditor a request for disposal of all unserviceable property
together with the Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property (IIRUP),
Appraisal Report and other required documents for appropriate action.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and the General Services Officer to reconcile the discrepancy between their records, and effect the necessary adjustments for fair presentation of the PPE account balances in the financial statement.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the designated General Supply Officer to update the Property Accountability Receipts (PAR) to facilitate the monitoring and control of assets and to establish accountability in case of transfer of accountable officer and loss of or damage to government properties.
observations.managementAction: Management surmised that the variance could have been the result of disposed equipment which were still recorded in the books, lost equipment, those not found on station and prior years’ balances that could no longer be identified. However, Management assured to continue tracking down the possible cause/s of the discrepancy. Updating of PAR shall also be taken into consideration.
observations.reason: No action to reconcile the deficiencies noted and to implement the recommend- ations were validated.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. Municipal Accountant
 to adopt the Perpetual Inventory Method in accounting for inventory
items, in compliance with Section 51 of MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I;
 to record the inventory issuances based on the Summary of Supplies and
Materials Issued (SSMI); and
 to maintain subsidiary ledger cards for each supplies and materials
for reconciliation with the stock cards of the Supply Officer.
b. Supply Officer
 to prepare and submit monthly the Report of Supplies and Materials
Issued (RSMI) to the Municipal Accountant for the recording of inventory
issuances;
 to maintain stock cards for supplies; and
 to prepare and submit the Report on the Physical Count of Inventories
(RPCI) to report the results of physical count of supplies.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct
the Municipal Accountant and the General Services Officer to reconcile the
discrepancy between their records, and effect the necessary adjustments for
fair presentation of the PPE account balances in the financial statement.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the
designated General Supply Officer to update the Property Accountability
Receipts (PAR) to facilitate the monitoring and control of assets and to
establish accountability in case of transfer of accountable officer and loss of
or damage to government properties.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor assign a personnel to
facilitate the transfer of titles of the acquired land in the name of the
Municipality of Lumban to protect the interest of the government.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal
Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to determine the causes of the
discrepancy amounting to P44,946.73 and make the necessary adjustments in
the books/records.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct both officers
to conduct periodic reconciliation of their records so that errors can be
promptly corrected, thus ensuring the fair presentation of the Cash in Bank-
Local Currency Current Account balance in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal
Accountant to make the necessary adjustment in the books of accounts, as
follows:

Account Title Dr Cr
Road Networks P108,798.00
Flood Control Systems 243,543.00
Prior Year’s P352,431.00
Adjustments

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that the
Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. Municipal Accountant
 to adopt the Perpetual Inventory Method in accounting for inventory
items, in compliance with Section 51 of MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I;
 to record the inventory issuances based on the Summary of Supplies
and Materials Issued (SSMI); and
 to maintain subsidiary ledger cards for each supplies and materials
for reconciliation with the stock cards of the Supply Officer.
b. Supply Officer
 to prepare and submit monthly the Report of Supplies and Materials
Issued (RSMI) to the Municipal Accountant for the recording of
inventory issuances;
 to maintain stock cards for supplies; and
 to prepare and submit the Report on the Physical Count of
Inventories (RPCI) to report the results of physical count of supplies.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the immediate
liquidation by concerned Municipal employees of the outstanding cash
advances totaling P139,637.94 in compliance with COA Circular Nos. 97-002.
Henceforth, see to it that all outstanding cash advances are fully liquidated at
the end of the year.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal
Accountant to determine the causes of the discrepancy between the
reciprocal accounts Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds and
make the necessary adjustments to bring both accounts in agreement, and, to
provide the required disclosures in the Notes to Financial Statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal
Accountant to issue demand letters to accountable officers to enforce the
payment of accounts receivables which have been long overdue.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal
Accountant to evaluate the possible applicability of COA Circular No. 2016-
005.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor create an Appraisal and
Disposal Committee to appraise and dispose the unserviceable property in
accordance with Section 79 of PD No. 1445.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct
the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator to monitor the timely
implementation of projects from the 20% Local Development Fund for the
benefits of the constituents.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal
Accountant, Municipal Budget Officer, and Municipal Planning and
Development Coordinator to implement only those programs/projects
embodied in the duly approved local development plan and refrain from
using the 20% LDF for purposes other than those provided under DILG-
DBM Joint Circular No. 2011-1.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal
Accountant to disclose the unexpended balance of the LDRRMF in the Notes
to Financial Statements in the manner described in Section 5.1.16 of COA
Circular No. 2012-002.

Accountant to record the unutilized balance of the LDRRMF under Trust
Liability 	6 DRRMF account.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Bids and Awards
Committee (BAC) Chairman to comply with Section 52, Rule XVI of RA No.
9184 in its procurement process to obtain the most advantageous price for the
government.

Municipal Treasurer to stamp PAID all Disbursement Vouchers and its
supporting documents so that possible double payment and other irregular
use of the documents could be prevented.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal
Accountant to prepare Disbursement Vouchers and the Municipal Treasurer
to draw checks directly in the name of the payee in compliance with Section
93 of PD No. 1445.

the MSWD officials to prepare a comprehensive plan translated into specific

kinds of abuse, exploitation and discrimination.

youth and the private sector be included in the LCPC membership.
